coredata.dc:title: Characterisation of corrosion products formed on PVD in situ mechanically worked Mg–Ti alloys
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Mitchell, T.
Diplas, S.
Tsakiropoulos, P.
coredata.dc:description: Mg–Ti alloys with up to 46wt.% Ti in solid solution, produced by PVD processing with in situ mechanical working, were subjected to immersion tests in 3wt.% NaCl solutions. The corrosion resistance of the alloys, assessed with gravimetric tests, was inferior to that of PVD pure Mg. Corrosive attack was favoured at the sites of mechanical working owing to the different microstructure and the higher Mg concentration at these areas. The corrosive attack resulted in exfoliation of the alloys. The corrosion product contained a mixture of MgO, TiO2 and some Mg(OH)2 at the outermost surface. Although Ti participation in the corrosion products was enhanced, its beneficial affect was overshadowed by the defective nature of the deposits. The surface chemistry of the alloys was studied using the Auger parameter.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Corrosion products
PVD
Mg–Ti alloys

xocs:meta.xocs:copyright-line: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterisation of the corroded surfaces via Auger parameter analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Planar (a) and cross-sectional ((b) and (c)) backscattered electron images of PVD Mg–Ti alloys after 7 days immersion in 3wt.% NaCl.

(a) Backscattered electron images of exfoliated corrosion products from the Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy. (b) A larger fragment already splitting into smaller fragments by exfoliation along the flail lines. The uncorroded areas in the fragments can be seen in (a).
TEM micrographs of Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy after immersion for 7 days in 3wt.% NaCl: (a) preferential attack of certain areas; (b) interface between the corrosion product and the alloy.

EDX maps of the Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy after 7 days immersion in 3wt.% NaCl. The analysis region was taken across the alloy/corrosion product interface. Contamination by silicon was attributed to specimen preparation.
EELS of the Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy after 7 days immersion in 3wt.% NaCl. The analysis area includes a corrosion product/alloy interface.

EELS of the Mg–46wt.% Ti alloy after 7 days immersion in 3wt.% NaCl. The corrosion product is almost homogeneous with a slight variation in Ti and has a needle-like morphology, as seen in the bright field image.

X-ray diffractogram of the Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy after immersion in 3wt.% NaCl for 7 days: (1) MgO; (2) Mg; (3) Mg(OH)2; (4) Mg; (5) Mg; (6) Mg; (7) Mg(OH)2; (8) Mg; (9) Mg(OH)2; (10) Mg; (11) Mg; (12) Mg(OH)2.
Variation of the pH of the 3wt.% NaCl solution during immersion tests of Mg–Ti alloys. The data for the Mg–46wt.% Ti is representative of all alloys with Ti content above 8wt.%.
Montage of XPS survey spectra of the Mg–40wt.% alloy after immersion in 3wt.% NaCl for 5min. Spectra representative of all alloys with Ti content above 8wt.%.

(a) Montage of Mg KLL spectra of the Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy after Ar+ sputtering (10min for each stage). (b) Montage of Mg KLL spectra of the Mg–26wt.% Ti alloy after Ar+ sputtering (1min for each stage). (c) Montage of Mg KLL spectra of the Mg–46wt.% Ti alloy after Ar+ sputtering (1min for each stage). Samples were studied after 5min immersion in 3wt.% NaCl.
Montage of Ti 2p spectra of the Mg–40wt.% Ti alloy corroded for 5min in 3wt.% NaCl. Spectra collected during Ar+ sputtering with etching time (a) 1min and (b) 10min per stage.
Montage of O 1s spectra of the Mg–40wt.% Ti alloy corroded for 5min in 3wt.% NaCl. Spectra collected during Ar+ sputtering with etching time 1min per stage.
Montage of Ti LMM spectra of the Mg–46wt.% Ti alloy corroded for 5min in 3wt.% NaCl. Spectra collected during Ar+ sputtering with etching time 10min.

(a) Mg KLL–1s Wagner plot showing lines of the final and initial state AP with slopes +1 and +3, respectively. The positions of Mg in the corrosion products of the Mg–46wt.% Ti alloy, as well as in MgO (literature value) and in Mg metal (both literature value and value from this study) are also shown. Figure representative of all alloys studied. (b) Ti LMM–2p Wagner plot showing lines of the final and initial state AP with slopes +1 and +3, respectively. The positions of Ti in the corrosion products of the Mg–46wt.% Ti alloy, as well as in TiO2 (literature value), in TiC and in Ti metal (literature values) are also shown. Figure representative of all alloys with Ti content above 8wt.%.
Corrosion rates of PVD Mg–Ti alloys in 3wt.% NaCl
Experimental parameters used on the VG Scientific Sigma Probe for the XPS analysis of the as-deposited and corroded alloys

article.ce:title: Characterisation of corrosion products formed on PVD in situ mechanically worked Mg–Ti alloys

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, University of Surrey, Guildford GU2 7XH, Surrey, UK

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Present address: TWI Ltd, Granta Park, Great Abington, Cambridge, UK.
Present address: Centre for Materials Science and Nanotechnology, University of Oslo, Gaustadalleen 21, NO-0349 Oslo, Norway.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mg–Ti alloys with up to 46wt.% Ti in solid solution, produced by PVD processing with in situ mechanical working, were subjected to immersion tests in 3wt.% NaCl solutions. The corrosion resistance of the alloys, assessed with gravimetric tests, was inferior to that of PVD pure Mg. Corrosive attack was favoured at the sites of mechanical working owing to the different microstructure and the higher Mg concentration at these areas. The corrosive attack resulted in exfoliation of the alloys. The corrosion product contained a mixture of MgO, TiO2 and some Mg(OH)2 at the outermost surface. Although Ti participation in the corrosion products was enhanced, its beneficial affect was overshadowed by the defective nature of the deposits. The surface chemistry of the alloys was studied using the Auger parameter.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decades, research on new materials has addressed the development of new Mg alloys. One of the aims of this research has been to increase the corrosion resistance of the new PVD Mg alloys in saline environments. Processing by non-equilibrium methods has been considered for the development of new alloys and some Mg–TM systems have been produced by PVD processing and evaluated for their corrosion properties [1–6].

Baldwin et al. [7,8] studied the corrosion behaviour of PVD Mg–Cr, Mg–Mn and Mg–Ti alloys. In addition to the standard gravimetric tests, open circuit potentials were measured, which gave information about changes in electrochemical activity. The lowest corrosion rate (0.3 mdd) was recorded for a Mg–44wt.% Ti alloy. Open circuit potentials (OCP) of the alloys showed that the addition of more noble elements such as Mn, Cr and Ti raised the OCP, i.e., the alloys become less electrochemically active. The results are significant in that the alloys produced should be less susceptible to galvanic corrosion than pure Mg. The explanation given for the increased corrosion resistance of the Mg–44Ti alloy, as compared to pure Mg, was that probably the Ti formed a protective surface film which acted in a similar manner to Cr in Fe–Cr systems, in that a network of metal–oxygen–metal linkages was formed on the alloy surface thereby suppressing the dissolution of the more active underlying Mg-rich matrix. These results and the fact that TiO2 is insoluble in aqueous solutions and is stable over a wide pH range suggested that further research would be required to establish whether Ti is a suitable alloying element for improving the corrosion resistance of new Mg alloys.

Preliminary research on PVD Mg–Ti alloys by Baliga et al. [9] used ion beam techniques to analyse the surface film formed on extended Mg(Ti) solid solutions of Mg–Ti alloys during corrosion. The results indicated the possibility of TiO2 forming on the surface of the alloys during processing. It was suggested that the corrosion resistance of PVD Mg–Ti alloys could be attributed to the formation of Ti spinels [10] during corrosion. However, no direct evidence was provided to support this.

This study has concentrated on the characterisation of corroded extended Mg(Ti) solid solutions of PVD Mg–Ti alloys that were subjected to in situ mechanical working (flailing). The in situ mechanical working aimed to reduce the porosity and destroy the columnar structure of the alloys, thus improving their corrosion performance. The alloys were studied after immersion in 3wt.% NaCl solution. The microstructure of the as-deposited in situ mechanically worked alloys has been described elsewhere [11]. To our knowledge, this is the first time that a study of the corrosion product formation on in situ mechanically worked PVD Mg–Ti alloys is reported.

The corrosion behaviour of the alloys given in Table 1 was assessed using total immersion corrosion tests in 3wt.% NaCl solution for 7 days. The corrosion products formed on the alloys were characterised by electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS).

Cross thickness sections of the corroded samples were mounted in resin and polished to a 1μm diamond finish prior to study under a Hitachi 3200N SEM operated at 10–20kV. Link Isis software was used to acquire multi-element X-ray maps in energy dispersive mode.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to study the corrosion products. The corroded samples were sandwiched together with silicon wafers to make a stable sample, and then they were glued to either copper or nickel grids and polished to 30–40μm thickness. The samples were subsequently ion beam milled to electron transparency using a Gatan dual ion mill 600 or a Gatan precision ion polishing system (PIPS). Ion beam milling was performed using a rotating stage and liquid nitrogen cooling to avoid over heating. The ion guns were set at an accelerating voltage of 5kV and the gun current was 1mA. The milling angle was set at 15° for the dual ion mill and at 4–6° for the PIPS system. TEM specimens of the exfoliated material were prepared by crushing dried corrosion products and then dispersing them in acetone. The dispersed corrosion products were dropped by a pipette onto carbon covered copper grids and were allowed to dry. TEM studies were performed on Phillips EM-400T and CM200 transmission electron microscopes operated at an accelerating voltage of 120 and 200kV, respectively.

PEELS elemental maps were obtained using a Philips CM200/STEM equipped with a Gatan imaging filter. The background subtraction was achieved using a three-window technique. Three energy filtered images were acquired, two pre-edge and one post-edge. An extrapolated background was calculated and subtracted from the ionisation edge image of the element of interest. The jump ratio method was used, where the ionisation edge image intensity was divided by the pre-edge image intensity.

The corrosion products were also analysed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a Phillips X-ray diffractometer with Cu radiation at a voltage of 40V and a current of 4mA with a step size of 0.02° in the range 20–120°. The samples were rinsed in distilled water and air-dried prior to XRD studies. The peak positions were compared with standard values obtained from the JCPDS data files. The pH changes of the corroding solution were measured at 293K using a Jenson 3020 pH meter. Standard buffer solutions of pH=7 and pH=10 were used to calibrate the pH electrode.

Surface analysis of samples immersed in 3wt.% NaCl solution for 5min was performed on a VG Scientific Sigma probe X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using an Al X-ray source (1486.6eV). The corroded samples were rinsed with deionised water and air-dried. For data acquisition and subsequent data processing, a computer running Sigma's Eclipse software Version 3.1 controlled the spectrometer. The spectra were obtained at a take off angle of 60° with respect to the surface of the sample. The peaks were fitted using Shirley background subtraction techniques and a suitable combination of fixed parameters. The spectral acquisition parameters are shown in Table 2 . Depth profiling was carried out in situ using Ar+ ion etching with an accelerating voltage of 3kV and a target current of 8μA. The etching time for each stage was either 1 or 10min.

The corrosion rates of all the PVD Mg–Ti alloys, which were determined from gravimetric corrosion rate measurements, exceeded the corrosion rate of PVD pure Mg (Table 1). The general trend was an increase of the corrosion rate as the alloying level of Ti in Mg was increased.

After immersion in 3wt.% NaCl the samples were rinsed in distilled water and inhibisol and then air-dried. Typical SEM images of the corroded samples in planar and cross-section orientation are shown in Fig. 1 . The corrosion product was layered and did not adhere to the substrate, thus becoming susceptible to exfoliation. In Fig. 1b and c the corrosive attack is concentrated around cracks and the sites of flailing. This type of attack caused exfoliation, thus exposing new uncorroded material. It is suggested that this is the most likely cause of the high corrosion rates of the in situ mechanically worked alloys in Table 1.

Exfoliated corrosion products analysed by SEM are shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a shows the uncorroded alloy fractured along a flailed region. The corrosive attack can be seen on the edges of the sample and the surface. Fig. 2b shows the cross-section of the alloy, which is covered by corrosion product. The flailed regions, which are beginning to separate, are also visible. Fig. 2 is representative of all the alloys studied.

TEM images of cross-sectional corroded samples are shown in Fig. 3 , which is representative of all alloys in this study. The results indicate that both sharp and diffuse interfaces between corroded and non-corroded regions were present. The pattern of corrosion products in Fig. 3a suggests that the corrosive attack had followed certain grain boundaries and branched out leaving some areas uncorroded. In Fig. 3b the corrosion product/alloy interface is less diffuse and a grain boundary can be seen.

EDX and EELS analyses of the areas shown in Fig. 3 as well as of the pure corrosion products are shown in Figs. 4–6
. The EDX elemental maps (Fig. 4) show the distribution of elements at the corrosion product/alloy interface and an enrichment of Ti at the corrosion product/alloy interface. The depletion of Mg concentration in the corrosion product compared to the alloy, as shown in Fig. 4, could be due to diffraction contrast in the image and/or MgO fluorescing. The corrosion product is also seen to be following grain boundaries or cracks in the uncorroded alloy (see the oxygen map in Fig. 4).

The EELS data for the Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy in Fig. 5 shows the zero loss images and the Mg, Ti and O jump ratios at the corrosion product/alloy interface. It can be seen that there is a titanium-rich layer at the outermost edge of the uncorroded alloy. Fig. 6 shows the EELS data only for the corrosion product region.

Fig. 7 shows the XRD diffractogram of the Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy after immersion in 3wt.% NaCl for 7 days. The main peaks belong to Mg, MgO, and Mg(OH)2 and are in agreement with previous work on Mg alloys. Fig. 8 shows the change in the pH of the 3wt.% NaCl solution during immersion of the Mg–8wt.% Ti and Mg–46wt.% Ti alloys over a period of 7 days. The pH of the corroding solution of the alloy with the lowest Ti content increased faster (at almost an order of magnitude) compared to the alloy with the highest Ti content.

The results of the XPS analyses of the surfaces of the Mg–Ti alloys after 5min immersion in 3wt.% NaCl are shown in Figs. 9–13
. These figures are representative of all alloys studied. Fig. 9 shows a montage of survey spectra taken from the Mg–46wt.% Ti alloy during depth profiling with Ar ion stuttering. The Ti 2p post-peak background slopes show an enrichment of the corrosion products in Ti. Montages of the acquired high resolution peaks from depth-profiled samples are shown in Figs. 10–13. Fig. 10 shows representative high resolution Mg KLL spectra with the metallic and oxide components. In Fig. 10b and c the metallic component is visible even in the spectra taken from the outermost surface whilst in Fig. 10a the Mg KLL peak consists only of the oxide component even after 60min etching. The spectra illustrate that the oxide layer formed on the surfaces of the alloys with Ti content above 8wt.% (Fig. 10b and c) was thinner than the oxide formed on the Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy (Fig. 10a). Fig. 11 shows that Ti in the corrosion scale was present in the TiO2 form. The measured peak positions 453±0.2eV for Ti (Fig. 11b) and 458.4±0.2eV for Ti oxide (Fig. 11a and b) are in very good agreement with the literature values given for Ti (453.89eV) and TiO2 (458.7eV) [12]. Moreover, the Ti 2p3/2–2p1/2 energy separation in the Ti oxide (Fig. 11a) was 5.7±0.3eV, which is also in good agreement with the literature value of 5.54eV [13]. Fig. 12 shows a representative montage of O 1s spectra of the Mg–40wt.% Ti alloy where the intensity of the hydroxide component decreases whilst the intensity of the oxide increases with depth, in agreement with previous works [3–5,14]. Fig. 13 shows a montage of the Ti LMM peak of the Mg–46wt.% Ti alloy during depth profile (10min sputtering for each stage). The figure shows the changes in the intensity of the metallic and oxide components with depth. Although not shown, the C 1s peak indicated presence of carbonates at the outermost surface thus suggesting hydromagnesite formation in agreement with previous studies [14].

The data in Table 1 would indicate that the corrosion rate of the PVD Mg–Ti alloys in 3wt.% NaCl increased with Ti addition. The data would also suggest that flailing had a marginal effect on the corrosion resistance of Mg–8wt.% Ti and perhaps had a significant effect on the corrosion resistance of alloys with more than 31wt.% Ti.

In pure water, the corrosion rate of Mg is low, as the air formed MgO hydrolyses to form a passivating layer of Mg(OH)2 [15]. In chlorine-containing solutions the corrosion is rapid, due to the solubility of Mg2+ in the corroding solution, which causes the pH of the corroding solution to rise. Once the pH has risen above the value for a saturated Mg(OH)2 solution in the Pourbaix diagram, formation of Mg(OH)2 on the surface can occur.

The corrosion of Mg is also governed by galvanic and micro-galvanic phenomena. Magnesium has a very low electrode potential (−2.73V for Mg(aq) 2+) and it would be expected to be consumed preferentially when in contact with a more noble element such as Ti (−0.37V for Ti(aq) 2+). In our case, the Ti was in solid solution with Mg and therefore micro-galvanic effects should not be expected to take place. However, the chemical inhomogeneity of the alloys in the form of bands with different Ti content [11] may give rise to galvanic and micro-galvanic effects.

The corrosive attack followed cracks into the alloy, and then branched out along the flailed areas causing the exfoliation of the alloy in layers (Fig. 1c). The high corrosion rates measured for the PVD Mg–Ti alloys in this study could be attributed to preferential attack and subsequent exfoliation of the corrosion products. The preferential attack is associated with the microstructure in the flailed regions, which was Mg-rich, heavily worked and contained small equiaxed grains and high dislocation densities [11]. The Mg-rich regions are expected to have more negative electrode potential than the adjacent regions, which are richer in Ti and therefore should corrode preferentially. A heavily worked region also offers energetically favourable sites for corrosion. Moreover, the presence of a large number of small equiaxed grains is associated with an increased total grain boundary area and therefore facilitates corrosive attack at the grain boundaries. Preferential attack of the flailed regions could also be attributed to Ti precipitates forming in the flailed areas causing micro-galvanic corrosion along these sites. However, precipitation of Ti in the flailed areas has not been confirmed by TEM [11]. Moreover, the flailed areas were depleted in Ti (i.e., they were Mg-rich) thus making Ti precipitation in these areas less probable. The most likely explanation is that the flailed areas are more susceptible to corrosion than the bulk of the alloy because of compositional and microstructural effects.

Despite the fact that the gravimetric tests suggested inferior corrosion behaviour of the Mg–Ti alloys compared to pure Mg (Table 1), Fig. 8 shows that during immersion tests the increase of the pH of the solution of the alloy with the highest Ti content was delayed compared to the alloy with the lowest Ti content. The pH increases as a result of Mg dissolution and subsequent Mg(OH)2 formation. The solution of the Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy reached a pH value close to the saturation level of the Mg(OH)2 solution (∼10.6) in a few minutes, whilst the solution of the Mg–46wt.% Ti alloy never reached the saturation level even after 7 days immersion.

As shown by the TEM studies, the metal/corrosion product interface was enriched in Ti oxide. In a corrosion process involving outward diffusion of cations one would expect the Ti-rich products at the metal/corrosion product interface not to play a particularly protective role towards a further advance of the corrosion process, since if they did then the thickness of the corrosion products formed above the Ti-rich interfacial regions would probably be very small. Evidence for inwards corrosion is given in Fig. 4 where penetration of corrosion beyond the oxide/metal interface is demonstrated by the oxygen elemental map. Thus, the relatively good compositional homogeneity of the corrosion products as well as the general form of the corroded alloys (see Figs. 1 and 3) would favour an inward corrosion process. However, despite the heavy attack at sites of compositional banding and mechanical working, the alloys seemed to maintain their pre-corrosion features (Fig. 1). It is possible that the corrosion process near the alloy/corrosion product interface was inhibited by the Ti-rich bands. This inhibition could be attributed to the more noble nature of the Mg(Ti) solid solution in the corrosion process as well as to the thermodynamic stability of Ti oxide in the corrosive environment [15]. Furthermore, the fact that the interfacial regions were also depleted in Mg makes the formation of a mixed spinel type oxide unlikely.

Fig. 10 demonstrates that the corrosion products formed on the surfaces of the alloys with Ti content above 8wt.% (Fig. 10b and c) had smaller thickness than the corrosion products formed on the surface of the Mg–8wt.% Ti alloy (Fig. 10a), under the same corrosion (5min immersion in NaCl) and analysis conditions. This is supported by the pH variation shown in Fig. 8. Titanium in the corrosion products was in the form of TiO2. The open circuit potential measurements for the PVD Mg–Ti alloys, using a standard calomel electrode, had values between −1.4 and −1.2V [7]. For this potential range the theoretical domain for Ti passivation lays above a pH range of 6–7, for passivation assumed to occur by anhydrous TiO2 oxide (rutile) [15]. For passivation assumed to occur by hydrated oxide (TiO2·H2O) the passivation domain extends above a pH range 7–8.5 [15]. This explains the deviation of the pH change between the two alloys for pH values above ∼7, as shown in Fig. 8. Above this value the surfaces of Mg–Ti alloys are probably passivated by TiO2. However, the defective nature of the deposits and the lack of homogeneity overshadow possible beneficial effects due to surface passivation via TiO2 formation.

The variation of the oxide and metallic components shown in Figs. 10, 11 and 13 would suggest a diffuse interface between the corrosion product and the alloy. A diffusive interface is also suggested by the TEM studies (Figs. 3–5) and it may be attributed to the fact that corrosion advances between the columnar grains of the alloys as well as the existence of metal oxide at the grain boundaries of the as-deposited alloys. Graded metal/oxide interfaces have been reported in previous studies on corroded PVD Mg–TM alloys [4,6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterisation of the corroded surfaces via Auger parameter analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The main strength of XPS is the identification of chemical states via shifts of the energies of the photoelectron and Auger peaks in the XPS spectrum. Changes in the core level binding energy may be interpreted as changes in the atomic core potential between two different environments. However, the accuracy of the measured energy values can be affected seriously by sample charging and energy referencing problems. Moreover, the binding energy shifts include both initial and final state contributions. The above difficulties can be overcome by using the final state Auger parameter (AP) [16,17]. In order to estimate initial state effects the concept of the initial state AP [18] can be used. The two parameters are given by Eqs. (1) and (2):
(1)
α = B
E
photoelectron
+ K
E
Auger
electron
(2)
β = K
E
( i j j )
+ B
E
( i )
+ 2 B
E
( j )
Eq. (1) shows that the final state AP α is obtained by adding the binding energy (BE) level of the photoelectron peak to the kinetic energy (KE) level of the equivalent Auger transition. In the definition of the initial state AP β, KE(ijj) is the kinetic energy of the Auger electron, whose emission involves the i and the j core levels, whilst BE(i) and BE(j) are the binding energies of the i and j levels, respectively.

The Auger parameter analysis requires the use of core sharp and well-localised transitions usually of the 1s–KLL or 2p–LMM type [19]. For the calculation of the Mg final state AP the Mg 1s and Mg KLL photoelectron and Auger peaks were used, respectively. However, a core–core–core Auger peak was not used for the calculation of the Ti final state AP. The Ti L3M23M45 Auger peak used in this work (Fig. 13) involves the valence band and complicates the analysis. However, the L3M23M45 is the strongest of the Ti LMM peaks. The position of this peak is mostly reported in the literature. Thus, we used this peak for comparison purposes. Fig. 14 a confirms the chemical state of Mg in the corrosion products of the Mg–Ti alloys, since it shows that MgO dominates in the oxide scale below the outermost surface (the oxide after 10 and 20min etching has the same final state AP with MgO).

Eq. (3) also expresses initial state effects [20]:
(3)
E k
= const . + 2 (
V M
+ k q ) − 3
E b
where E k and E b are the kinetic and binding energies, respectively, V M is the local Madelung potential, k the change of the core potential when a valence electron is removed and q a negative quantity representing the ground-state valence charge. Eq. (3) is an alternative expression of the initial state AP defined by Eq. (2).

Following Moretti [20], we can use Eqs. (2) and (3) to calculate the difference in the intercepts with the y-axis (see Fig. 14a and b) between the metal (Mg) and the oxide (MgO) state:
(4)
Δ
E k
( MgO – Mg ) = [
E k
( MgO ) + 3
E b
( MgO ) ] − [
E k
( Mg ) + 3
E b
( Mg ) ]
The Madelung potential and the ground-state charge can be incorporated by combining Eqs. (3) and (4):
(5)
[
V M
( MgO ) −
V M
( Mg ) ] + [ k q ( MgO ) −
k 0
q 0
( Mg ) ] =
1 2
Δ
E k
( MgO – Mg )
In the case of cations |q|<|q 0|, where q and q 0 are the valence charges of the Mg ion and metal, respectively, V M is always a negative quantity,
V M
( MgO ) ≫
V M
( Mg )
and the term [kq(MgO)− k 0 q 0(Mg)] is always positive [20]. Therefore, for negative ΔE k(MgO–Mg) values (also negative differences in the initial state AP between metal and oxide), such as in the case of Mg and MgO (Fig. 14a), we must have
(6)
| [
V M
( MgO ) −
V M
( Mg ) ] | > | [ k q ( MgO ) −
k 0
q 0
( Mg ) ] |
Relations (5) and (6) show that the more negative the shift ΔE k(MgO–Mg), the more negative the V M becomes. Thus, in terms of initial state effects the position of MgO on the graph is largely determined by the negative Madelung potential for Mg in MgO.

The position of Mg in the alloy corrosion products in Fig. 14a is such that ΔE k(MgO–Mg)≈0 especially for the outermost oxide. In this case Eq. (5) takes the form
(7)
[
V M
( MgO ) −
V M
( Mg ) ] = − k q ( MgO ) +
k 0
q 0
( Mg )
Eq. (7) indicates that the more ionic the character of the bond is
| q | ≪ |
q 0
|
, the more negative the local Madelung potential becomes. This could suggest that Mg atoms, in the alloy corrosion products and especially in the outermost surface, participate in less ionic chemical bonds compared to the bonds in MgO. This could possibly be attributed to the domination of Mg hydroxide in the outermost layers. Fig. 14a confirms that the chemical state of the Mg below the outermost surface is the same with Mg in MgO. The initial AP also suggests that the chemical bond in the oxide is probably less ionic compared to pure MgO. However, the last statement should be treated with caution. Although the final state AP is independent of energy referencing problems, the initial state AP shows some energy dependence due to the triple weighing of the binding energy values.

In Fig. 14b the initial state AP of Ti in the oxides is higher than the pure Ti one. Since ΔE k(TiO2–Ti)>0 the term [kq(TiO2)− k 0 q 0(Ti)] must be very positive, i.e.,
| q | ≪ |
q 0
|
. This is interpreted as an indication of a chemical bond of increased ionisity [20].

The Ti final state AP of the outermost surface has the same values as TiO2. However, it acquires different values below the outermost surface. Furthermore, the initial state AP seems to acquire different values for TiO2 and also for the Ti on the surfaces revealed after several sputtering stages. The main reason for this is the difficulty to estimate precisely the energy of the oxide component of the Ti LMM peak. The fact that the LMM Auger transition involves the valence band also complicates the interpretation in terms of the AP relation to relaxation or screening effects. It should be also noted that the estimation of the LMM value for the oxide compound relies upon deconvolution of the broad LMM peak (see Fig. 13), which was less accurate as the oxide component decreased in intensity.

The corrosion of PVD in situ mechanically worked Mg–Ti alloys with 8wt.%<Ti<46wt.% was inferior to that of PVD pure Mg. The increased corrosion has been attributed to preferential attack along compositional banding, the sites of mechanical working and to the exfoliation of the alloys. The corrosion product contained a mixture of MgO, TiO2 and some Mg(OH)2 at the outermost surface. It would appear that in situ mechanical working is not beneficial to the corrosion behaviour of PVD Mg alloys, since it has a local effect and leads to the formation of narrow regions of worked microstructure surrounded by large regions with the typical PVD microstructure. The presence of defects in the inhomogeneous microstructure of the alloys overshadowed possible beneficial effects such as surface passivation due to Ti presence. The surface chemistry of the corroded alloys can be studied using the Auger parameter.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We wish to thank Mr. Steve Greeves for assistance with XPS, Dr. R. Brydson for his help with EELS and Mr. R. Gardiner and Dr. S. Dodd for assistance with the processing of the alloys. Financial support to one of us (TM) by the EPSRC is gratefully acknowledged.
